faq.sidebarSub: Reach out directly. We typically respond within 2 hours during working hours.

items.q: What is the first step if an FIR is filed against me?
items.a: Under Indian law, the first step is typically to obtain a certified copy of the FIR, as it is a public document available from the police station or the state police website. Legal counsel can then review the allegations and, where custody is a risk, prepare a bail application. Responding through a lawyer rather than directly to the investigating officer is the typical practice at this stage.

items.q: How is anticipatory bail different from regular bail?
items.a: Anticipatory bail is protection sought under Section 438 CrPC before an arrest, typically when a person apprehends arrest in a non-bailable offence. Regular bail, by contrast, is applied for after arrest or during trial before the court holding jurisdiction. The two remedies apply at different stages of a criminal proceeding.

items.q: What documents are required for mutual consent divorce?
items.a: Mutual consent divorce under the Hindu Marriage Act typically requires the marriage certificate, proof of residence, income and asset details of both parties, and a settlement agreement covering maintenance and custody where applicable. Both spouses file a joint petition affirming their consent. Courts generally require a minimum period of separation before proceedings can be initiated.

items.q: How long does a property dispute case usually take?
items.a: Duration depends on factors such as the complexity of title records, the number of parties involved, and the court's caseload. Straightforward possession or partition matters typically conclude within one to three years, while disputes involving multiple appeals can take considerably longer. Timelines remain case-specific.

items.q: Can police enter my house without a warrant?
items.a: Under the CrPC, police generally require a warrant to enter and search a private residence, except in defined circumstances such as a cognizable offence in progress, hot pursuit of a suspect, or a reasonable apprehension that evidence may be destroyed. These exceptions are set out in law and subject to procedural safeguards. Entry outside these exceptions is not standard procedure.

items.q: What is the difference between an FIR and a police complaint?
items.a: An FIR is registered under Section 154 CrPC specifically for cognizable offences and sets the criminal justice process in motion. A police complaint may instead relate to a non-cognizable offence and typically does not, by itself, authorise investigation without magistrate approval. The two documents follow different procedural pathways.

items.q: How is child custody decided in Indian courts?
items.a: Courts primarily consider the welfare and best interest of the child, taking into account factors such as the child's age, emotional bond with each parent, and stability of the home environment. Custody matters are typically governed by the Hindu Minority and Guardianship Act or the Guardians and Wards Act, depending on the case. Arrangements can be sole, joint, or visitation-based.

items.q: Is a lawyer necessary during police station questioning?
items.a: Under Article 22 of the Constitution, a person has the right to consult a legal practitioner of their choice, and this extends to the period of police questioning. The law does not mandate a lawyer's physical presence in every instance, but legal representation is a recognised safeguard against procedural irregularities. Applicable rules can vary with the offence and stage of investigation.

items.q: What courts does Advocate Sourabh Rawat practise in?
items.a: Advocate Sourabh Rawat practises at the District & Sessions Court in Lucknow and the High Court of Judicature at Allahabad, Lucknow Bench. His practice covers criminal, civil, and family law matters before these courts.

firQuashSection.title: FIR Quashing in Lucknow
firQuashSection.content: Under Section 528 BNSS (erstwhile Section 482 CrPC), the High Court of Judicature at Allahabad, Lucknow Bench has inherent jurisdiction to quash an FIR where the allegations are prima facie not made out or constitute an abuse of process. Applications for FIR quashing are filed before the High Court Lucknow Bench. Grounds commonly considered include: settlement between parties in matrimonial disputes, absence of cognizable offence, and mala fide registration.
steps: Filing a criminal misc. writ petition before High Court Lucknow Bench
Seeking interim stay on arrest or investigation during pendency
Final hearing on merits of quashing application
firQuashSection.note: Each case is assessed on its individual facts. The information above is general in nature and does not constitute legal advice.

bailGuide.title: Bail Process in Lucknow Courts
bailGuide.content: Bail in Lucknow is applied for before different courts depending on the stage and nature of the offence. For bailable offences, bail may be granted at the police station itself — commonly referred to as station bail. For non-bailable offences, a bail application is filed before the Chief Judicial Magistrate (CJM) or the Sessions Court, District Court Lucknow. If bail is rejected at the Sessions Court level, the accused may approach the High Court of Judicature at Allahabad, Lucknow Bench by filing a Criminal Misc. Bail Application. Where arrest is anticipated but has not yet taken place, anticipatory bail under Section 482 BNSS (erstwhile Section 438 CrPC) may be applied for before the Sessions Court or the High Court Lucknow Bench. Courts in Lucknow consider factors including the nature of the offence, criminal antecedents, likelihood of the accused absconding, and potential tampering with evidence while deciding bail applications. If a chargesheet is not filed by the police within 60 days (for offences punishable up to 7 years) or 90 days (for offences punishable with death, life imprisonment, or imprisonment exceeding 7 years), the accused becomes entitled to default bail under Section 187 BNSS.

firGuide.title: How to File an FIR in Lucknow
firGuide.content: An FIR (First Information Report) is the formal written record prepared by police upon receiving information about a cognizable offence — governed under Section 173 of the Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (BNSS). In Lucknow, an FIR may be filed at the jurisdictional police station — the station within whose limits the offence occurred. Lucknow Commissionerate Police has multiple police stations across zones including Madiyaon, Kaiserbagh, Hazratganj, Gomti Nagar, and others. The complainant must approach the duty officer or Station House Officer (SHO) and provide details of the incident orally or in writing. If provided orally, the officer is legally required to reduce it to writing, read it back, and obtain the complainant's signature. A copy of the registered FIR must be provided to the complainant free of charge. If the police refuse to register an FIR, the complainant may approach the Superintendent of Police, Lucknow, or file a complaint under Section 175(3) BNSS before the Magistrate of First Class. For certain offences, UP Police also provides the facility to file an FIR through the UP Police citizen portal (uppolice.gov.in). A Zero FIR may be filed at any police station in Lucknow regardless of jurisdiction and transferred to the concerned station thereafter.

The Madiyaon area of Lucknow, where one of the chambers is located, falls under the jurisdiction of Civil Judge (Senior Division), Lucknow.

propertyGuide.title: Filing a Property Dispute Case in Lucknow
propertyGuide.content: Property disputes in Lucknow are primarily adjudicated before the Civil Court Lucknow — comprising the Court of Civil Judge (Junior Division) for suits valued up to ₹1 lakh, and the Court of Civil Judge (Senior Division) for higher-value matters. Appeals lie to the High Court of Judicature at Allahabad, Lucknow Bench. A civil suit for property disputes typically begins with the filing of a plaint setting out the cause of action, the relief sought, and the valuation of the suit. Common reliefs sought in property disputes include declaration of title, permanent injunction restraining interference with possession, mandatory injunction for restoration of possession, and partition of jointly-owned property. An application for a temporary injunction or stay order — seeking to maintain the status quo pending disposal of the suit — may be filed alongside the plaint and is heard at an early stage. Property disputes in areas such as Madiyaon, Aliganj, Gomti Nagar, Hazratganj, and Indira Nagar fall within the jurisdiction of District Court Lucknow. The limitation period for filing a civil suit for possession based on title is 12 years from the date the right to sue accrues, and for recovery of money it is generally 3 years.

The court is located in the Civil Court complex, Lucknow. divorceGuide.title: Divorce Procedure at Family Court Lucknow
divorceGuide.content: Divorce petitions in Lucknow are filed before the Family Court, Lucknow, established under the Family Courts Act, 1984, and located within the Civil Court complex. For Hindus, divorce is governed by the Hindu Marriage Act, 1955; for Muslims, the applicable personal law applies; and for inter-religious marriages, the Special Marriage Act, 1954 applies. Mutual consent divorce under Section 13-B of the Hindu Marriage Act requires both spouses to file a joint petition, have been living separately for at least one year, and appear for two separate hearings — the first motion and the second motion — with a statutory cooling-off period of six months in between, which may be waived by the Family Court Lucknow in appropriate circumstances. Contested divorce, filed on grounds such as cruelty, desertion, adultery, or mental disorder, involves filing a petition, service of notice on the respondent, filing of written statements, and trial on merits. Child custody, maintenance under Section 125 CrPC / BNSS, and domestic violence proceedings under the Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act, 2005 are also dealt with by the Family Court Lucknow and related courts. Documents typically required include the marriage certificate, proof of address, identity proof, and photographs of both parties.

police-station.content: The crucial moments during a police inquiry can determine the trajectory of your case. rightsGuide.title: Your Legal Rights During Police Questioning in Uttar Pradesh
rightsGuide.content: Under Indian law and the Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (BNSS), individuals subject to police questioning or arrest at police stations in Lucknow and across Uttar Pradesh have defined legal rights. Upon arrest, the police are required to inform the arrested person of the grounds of arrest and their right to be released on bail where applicable. An arrested person cannot be detained for more than 24 hours without being produced before a Magistrate — this is a fundamental right under Article 22 of the Constitution of India. The arrested person has the right to have one person — a friend, relative, or nominated person — informed of their arrest and the place of detention. Every arrested person has the right to consult a legal practitioner of their choice. Under the BNSS, a woman cannot be arrested after sunset and before sunrise except in exceptional circumstances and only with the written permission of a Judicial Magistrate of First Class. Women are not required to appear at a police station for questioning — statements may be recorded at their residence. In Lucknow, if rights are violated during arrest or questioning, a complaint may be made before the Magistrate or the Senior Superintendent of Police (SSP), Lucknow.

criminal-law.q: What is the difference between bailable and non-bailable offences?
criminal-law.a: Bailable offences allow bail to be granted as a matter of right by the police or the court, while non-bailable offences require a judicial application and the exercise of court discretion. Classification is determined under the First Schedule of the CrPC based on the severity of the offence. This distinction affects how quickly a person can secure release after arrest.

criminal-law.q: Can an FIR be quashed by the High Court?
criminal-law.a: An FIR can be quashed by the High Court under Section 482 CrPC if it is found to be frivolous, malicious, or if the allegations, even taken at face value, do not disclose a cognizable offence. Quashing petitions are filed directly before the High Court and decided on the specific facts of each case. This remedy is typically sought in cases of alleged false implication.

criminal-law.q: What happens after arrest during a criminal investigation?
criminal-law.a: The accused must typically be produced before a magistrate within 24 hours, excluding travel time, as mandated under Article 22 of the Constitution. The magistrate may then authorise police custody or judicial custody depending on the requirements of the investigation. Bail applications can be filed at this stage before the appropriate court.

criminal-law.q: What is the procedure for a sessions court trial?
criminal-law.a: A sessions court trial typically involves framing of charges, recording of prosecution and defence evidence, cross-examination of witnesses, final arguments, and judgment. The process follows the procedure laid out under the CrPC for warrant trials. Duration varies with the number of witnesses and the complexity of the case.

criminal-law.q: What are NDPS Act cases and how are they different?
criminal-law.a: Cases under the Narcotic Drugs and Psychotropic Substances Act carry stringent bail provisions under Section 37, which requires the court to be satisfied that the accused is unlikely to commit an offence while on bail. These cases generally involve a higher threshold for securing bail compared to ordinary criminal matters. Sentencing is also typically more severe for offences involving commercial quantities.

criminal-law.q: Can a criminal case be settled through compromise?
criminal-law.a: Certain offences are compoundable under Section 320 CrPC, meaning the complainant and accused can reach a settlement with court permission, resulting in the case being closed. Serious or non-compoundable offences cannot be resolved this way even if both parties agree. Whether compounding is available depends on the specific offence charged.

criminal-law.q: What is the role of a criminal appeal in the High Court?
criminal-law.a: A criminal appeal allows a convicted person to challenge a trial court's judgment before the High Court on questions of fact or law. The appellate court reviews the evidence and legal reasoning of the lower court and may confirm, modify, or set aside the conviction or sentence. Appeals are typically filed within the limitation period prescribed under the CrPC.

criminal-law.q: What is the difference between POCSO cases and general criminal cases?
criminal-law.a: Cases under the Protection of Children from Sexual Offences Act follow a specialised procedure, including trial before designated POCSO courts, child-friendly examination processes, and time-bound trial provisions. These procedural safeguards differ from those applicable in general criminal trials. Bail provisions under POCSO are also generally more stringent.

criminal-law.q: What is the process for regular bail in Sessions Court?
criminal-law.a: An application for regular bail is typically filed before the Sessions Court after arrest, supported by grounds such as the nature of the allegations, cooperation with investigation, and absence of flight risk. The court examines the case diary and hears both the defence and the prosecution before deciding. Conditions such as sureties or reporting requirements may be attached if bail is granted.

criminal-law.q: Can economic offences like cheque bounce lead to imprisonment?
criminal-law.a: Cheque bounce cases under Section 138 of the Negotiable Instruments Act can result in imprisonment of up to two years, a fine, or both, and are typically tried in a summary manner. The provision is compoundable, allowing for settlement between the parties. Procedure and evidence requirements differ from those in general criminal trials.

criminal-law.q: How long does anticipatory bail take from Lucknow High Court?
criminal-law.a: Anticipatory bail applications filed before the High Court Lucknow Bench are typically listed for hearing within a few days of filing. Where the urgency is clearly indicated, courts may list the matter on shorter notice. The application is decided after the State is heard — usually within two to four hearings depending on the court's schedule and the complexity of the matter. An interim stay on arrest may also be sought pending the final decision.

criminal-law.q: Can I get bail on the same day of arrest in Lucknow?
criminal-law.a: In cases involving bailable offences, station bail may be granted at the police station itself — often within a few hours of arrest. For non-bailable offences, a bail application is filed before the Magistrate or Sessions Court after production and may be heard the same working day if filed promptly and the court schedule permits. Same-day outcomes depend on the nature of the offence, the court's docket, and the urgency communicated to the court.

criminal-law.q: What is the legal fee for a criminal lawyer in Lucknow?
criminal-law.a: Legal fees in criminal matters vary based on the nature of the case, the stage of proceedings, and whether the matter is before a Magistrate, Sessions Court, or the High Court Lucknow Bench. Advocate Sourabh Rawat follows a fully transparent fee structure — the complete fee is discussed and agreed before any work is commenced. There are no hidden charges or bills beyond what is agreed at the outset.

family-law.q: What is the difference between mutual consent and contested divorce?
family-law.a: Mutual consent divorce is filed jointly by both spouses who agree to end the marriage and settle issues such as maintenance and custody by consent. Contested divorce is filed by one spouse on statutory grounds such as cruelty or desertion, with the other party permitted to oppose the petition. The two processes differ significantly in procedure and typical timelines.

family-law.q: How is maintenance calculated under Indian law?
family-law.a: Maintenance under Section 125 CrPC or the Hindu Marriage Act is typically assessed based on factors such as the income and earning capacity of both spouses, the standard of living during the marriage, and the needs of any dependent children. Courts have discretion in determining the amount, and no fixed formula applies uniformly. Both interim and final maintenance can be awarded at different stages of proceedings.

family-law.q: What is required to file for restitution of conjugal rights?
family-law.a: A petition under Section 9 of the Hindu Marriage Act is filed when one spouse has withdrawn from the other's company without reasonable cause. The petitioner must show that cohabitation has ceased and that no valid justification exists for the withdrawal. Courts may pass a decree directing resumption of cohabitation if the claim is established.

family-law.q: What protections does the Domestic Violence Act provide?
family-law.a: The Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act, 2005 provides for protection orders, residence orders, and monetary relief for women facing physical, emotional, sexual, or economic abuse within a domestic relationship. Applications are typically filed before a Magistrate under this Act. Interim orders can also be sought while proceedings are pending.

family-law.q: How is custody different from guardianship?
family-law.a: Custody generally refers to the day-to-day care and physical possession of a child, while guardianship under the Guardians and Wards Act encompasses broader legal responsibility for the child's welfare, property, and decision-making. A person can be a guardian without having physical custody. Courts typically address both aspects when determining arrangements involving minors.

family-law.q: Can a divorce be granted without the other spouse's consent?
family-law.a: A contested divorce can be granted without the other spouse's consent if the petitioner establishes statutory grounds such as cruelty, desertion, or adultery under the applicable personal law. The court examines evidence presented by both sides before deciding whether these grounds are made out. This process typically takes longer than a mutual consent divorce.

family-law.q: What is the minimum separation period for mutual consent divorce?
family-law.a: Under the Hindu Marriage Act, couples seeking mutual consent divorce typically need to show they have lived separately for at least one year before filing. Courts may waive the standard cooling-off period between the first and second motions in certain circumstances, as clarified by judicial precedent. Requirements can vary based on the personal law applicable to the parties.

Family court proceedings, particularly those involving matrimonial disputes and child custody, are generally conducted with a degree of privacy, and courts may restrict public access to sensitive details. This is intended to protect the interests of the parties and any children involved. Specific confidentiality protocols can vary by court and case type.

family-law.q: What documents are needed to file for child maintenance?
family-law.a: An application for child maintenance typically requires proof of the child's birth and relationship to the parents, income details of both parents, and evidence of the child's educational or medical expenses. The application can be filed under Section 125 CrPC or the Hindu Adoption and Maintenance Act, depending on the circumstances. Courts assess the needs of the child alongside the paying parent's capacity.

family-law.q: How long does divorce take at Family Court Lucknow?
family-law.a: Mutual consent divorce at Family Court Lucknow can typically be concluded within six months to one year, including the statutory waiting period between the first and second motions — which the court may waive in appropriate cases. Contested divorce proceedings generally take longer, commonly one to three years depending on the issues in dispute and the court's schedule. These timelines are estimates and actual duration is case-specific.

family-law.q: Can interim maintenance be granted at the first or second hearing?
family-law.a: An application for interim maintenance, filed alongside the main petition, is heard by the court at an early stage after notice is served on the opposite party. Courts may grant interim maintenance after brief arguments from both sides — this can sometimes occur within the first few hearings depending on the disclosed income of both parties and the needs of the applicant. Interim maintenance is intended to provide financial support during the pendency of proceedings.

family-law.q: Can a wife living in Lucknow file for divorce if the husband is in another city?
family-law.a: Under Indian law, a divorce petition may be filed before the Family Court at the place where the parties last resided together, where the wife currently resides, or where the marriage was solemnised. A wife residing in Lucknow may file her petition before Family Court Lucknow even if the respondent husband resides elsewhere, subject to proper jurisdiction being established. The court then arranges service of notice on the respondent.

civil-law.q: What is the difference between a civil suit and a writ petition?
civil-law.a: A civil suit is filed before a district or subordinate court to resolve disputes between private parties, such as property or contract matters, under the Code of Civil Procedure. A writ petition is filed directly before the High Court under Article 226 of the Constitution, typically to challenge government action or protect fundamental rights. The two remedies serve different purposes and are heard by different forums.

civil-law.q: What is a stay order and when is it granted?
civil-law.a: A stay order is an interim direction that temporarily halts a specific action, such as construction, dispossession, or enforcement of a decree, while the underlying dispute is being decided. Courts typically grant a stay when there is a prima facie case and the balance of convenience favours the applicant. Stay orders can be temporary or made permanent at the conclusion of proceedings.

civil-law.q: How does property partition work among family members?
civil-law.a: Property partition allows co-owners, typically family members holding an undivided share, to divide a property into individually owned portions. This can be done through mutual agreement recorded in a partition deed or, where consensus is not possible, through a partition suit filed in civil court. Courts examine title documents and the respective shares of each party before passing a partition decree.

civil-law.q: What is the limitation period for filing a recovery suit?
civil-law.a: Under the Limitation Act, 1963, a suit for recovery of money is typically required to be filed within three years from the date the cause of action arises, such as the date of default or the last acknowledgment of debt. Filing after this period may result in the claim being barred unless a valid exception applies. Limitation periods vary depending on the nature of the claim.

civil-law.q: What is Section 138 of the Negotiable Instruments Act?
civil-law.a: Section 138 makes the dishonour of a cheque due to insufficient funds a punishable offence, provided statutory notice is issued to the drawer and payment is not made within the prescribed period. It combines civil recovery objectives with criminal consequences. Complaints under this section are typically filed before a Magistrate.

civil-law.q: What is required to prove ownership in a property dispute?
civil-law.a: Ownership is typically established through documents such as the sale deed, mutation records, property tax receipts, and revenue records reflecting continuous possession. Courts examine the chain of title and supporting documentary evidence to determine rightful ownership. The specific documents required can vary depending on the history of the property.

civil-law.q: Can a civil dispute be resolved through mediation instead of trial?
civil-law.a: Civil disputes can be referred to mediation under Section 89 of the Code of Civil Procedure, either at the request of the parties or on the court's direction, as an alternative to a full trial. Mediation is typically faster and allows parties to arrive at a mutually acceptable settlement. Not all matters are suited to mediation, and courts assess this on a case-by-case basis.

civil-law.q: How do I stop illegal construction on disputed property in Lucknow?
civil-law.a: An application for a temporary injunction restraining construction may be filed before the Civil Court Lucknow alongside the main civil suit. Courts examine whether there is a prima facie case in the applicant's favour, whether the balance of convenience supports granting the order, and whether irreparable harm would result without immediate relief. In urgent cases, an ex-parte ad-interim order may be passed on the date of filing itself — halting construction before the opposite party is heard.

civil-law.q: How quickly can a stay order be obtained in Lucknow Civil Court?
civil-law.a: An ex-parte ad-interim stay — a temporary measure granted without initially hearing the opposite party — may be passed on the date the application is filed if the court is satisfied that the matter is urgent and a prima facie case exists. A regular temporary injunction is decided after the opposite party is heard, which typically takes a few hearings. In urgent matters, applications are generally listed for hearing within the same week.

civil-law.q: Can I claim compensation for someone illegally occupying my property in Lucknow?
civil-law.a: A suit for recovery of mesne profits — compensation for unlawful use and occupation of property — may be filed before the Civil Court Lucknow alongside or after a suit for possession. Courts assess mesne profits based on the fair rental value of the property during the period of unlawful occupation. The claim can extend from the date of unlawful dispossession up to the date actual possession is restored.

police-station.q: What rights does a person have immediately after arrest?
police-station.a: Under Article 22 of the Constitution, a person arrested has the right to be informed of the grounds of arrest, the right to consult a legal practitioner of their choice, and the right to be produced before a magistrate within 24 hours. These safeguards apply regardless of the offence alleged. Detention beyond this period without magisterial authorisation is not permitted under law.

police-station.q: What happens if police refuse to register an FIR?
police-station.a: If police refuse to register an FIR for a cognizable offence, the complainant can approach the Superintendent of Police under Section 154(3) CrPC or apply before a Magistrate under Section 156(3) CrPC directing registration. These are the statutory remedies available when registration is denied. Only a cognizable offence needs to be disclosed at the registration stage.

police-station.q: How long can police detain a person without formal arrest?
police-station.a: Police may question a person during a preliminary inquiry, but formal detention without arrest and production before a magistrate is not permitted beyond a reasonable period under the CrPC. Once arrested, production before a magistrate is required within 24 hours, excluding travel time. Prolonged detention outside this procedure would not be in accordance with law.

police-station.q: What is Section 41 CrPC and when can police arrest without a warrant?
police-station.a: Section 41 CrPC allows police to arrest without a warrant in specific circumstances, such as when a cognizable offence has been committed in their presence or there is credible information and reasonable suspicion of involvement in a cognizable offence. The provision also requires police to record reasons for the arrest. Arrests outside these defined circumstances typically require a warrant.

police-station.q: Can a statement recorded by police be used as evidence in court?
police-station.a: A statement made to police during investigation under Section 161 CrPC is generally not admissible as substantive evidence and cannot be used to convict a person, though it may be used to contradict a witness during trial. Confessions made to police officers are separately inadmissible under the Indian Evidence Act, subject to limited exceptions. Statements recorded before a magistrate under Section 164 CrPC carry different evidentiary value.

police-station.q: What immediate steps are available in an emergency police matter?
police-station.a: In an emergency police matter, such as apprehension of unlawful detention or an urgent FIR situation, contacting a legal practitioner promptly and documenting the details of the incident are typical first steps. Emergency legal assistance can also be sought to help ensure procedural safeguards are followed at the police station. Availability of immediate assistance can vary with the specific circumstances.

police-station.q: What should I do if I receive a police notice (Section 41A) in Lucknow?
police-station.a: A notice under Section 41A CrPC (now Section 35 BNSS) requires a person to appear before the police for questioning without a formal arrest. Appearing as directed is advisable, and bringing a lawyer is legally permissible. Non-compliance without reasonable cause may give the police grounds to proceed with arrest. Consulting a lawyer before attending or responding to such a notice is strongly recommended.

police-station.q: How do I get a family member released from police custody in Lucknow?
police-station.a: If a family member has been arrested at a Lucknow police station, a lawyer can file a bail application before the competent court — typically the Chief Judicial Magistrate (CJM) or Sessions Court, Lucknow, depending on the offence. If the detention appears illegal or bail is unavailable, a writ of habeas corpus may be filed before the High Court Lucknow Bench seeking immediate production and release. Acting promptly is critical as procedural timelines apply.

police-station.q: What are my legal options if falsely implicated in a case in Lucknow?
police-station.a: If falsely implicated, the options available include: filing for anticipatory bail to prevent arrest, filing a complaint of false implication before a Magistrate, and — after arrest — seeking regular bail before the competent court. In appropriate cases, an application for quashing the FIR may be filed before the High Court Lucknow Bench under Section 528 BNSS. Securing legal representation at the earliest stage significantly affects outcomes.

team.sourabhDesc: With over 10 years of practice at the High Court and District Courts, Adv. Sourabh Rawat leads the chambers with expertise in criminal defense and civil litigation.
team.assoc1Desc: Expertise in handling complex family law disputes and civil matters with a strategic and compassionate approach.

about.meta_title: About — Adv. Sourabh Rawat | Advocate High Court Lucknow

about.name: Adv. Sourabh Rawat
about.enrollment: Enrolled — Bar Council of Uttar Pradesh
about.bio_1: Advocate Sourabh Rawat is enrolled with the Bar Council of Uttar Pradesh and practises across District Court Lucknow and the High Court of Judicature at Allahabad, Lucknow Bench.
about.bio_2: His practice covers criminal, civil, and family law matters. With over a decade of practice, he has represented clients across a range of proceedings in Lucknow courts.
about.bio_3: Consultations are available at both chamber locations during practice hours. Initial queries are responded to within two hours.
